model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: UConn holds a commanding advantage in this 2-vs-3 seed Sweet 16 matchup, with an adjusted efficiency margin of approximately +29.0 compared to Michigan State's estimated +15-16, a roughly 13-point gap per 100 possessions driven by UConn's elite defense (AdjD 94.0, 11th nationally) that should severely limit Michigan State's below-average offense (ORtg 117.3). The Huskies' balanced attack on both ends of the floor creates a significant two-way mismatch against a Michigan State team whose defensive rating of 101.7 will struggle to contain UConn's offensive firepower led by Tarris Reed Jr. and Alex Karaban. While Tom Izzo's legendary March pedigree and his 4-3 historical record against UConn give Michigan State a credible upset path, the quality gap is too wide for the Spartans to overcome, and UConn's health situation appears manageable with Silas Demary Jr. healthy and Jaylin Stewart expected to return.

reasoningStep.title: East Sweet 16 2: Michigan State vs UConn
reasoningStep.summary: UConn's elite two-way profile and roughly 13-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage over Michigan State make the Huskies a comfortable favorite despite Izzo's March track record.
evidence: UConn ranks 9th nationally with an AdjEM of +29.0, featuring a top-11 defense (AdjD 94.0) and strong offense (~AdjO 123)
Michigan State's metrics are more modest for a 3-seed (ORtg 117.3, DRtg 101.7), creating a roughly 13-point efficiency gap per 100 possessions
UConn's 29-5 record under Dan Hurley reflects championship-caliber consistency, chasing a third national title in four years
Michigan State's SRS of 23.44 and Big Ten schedule strength (8th nationally) are respectable but insufficient to close the quality gap
UConn injury concerns (Jaylin Stewart, Silas Demary Jr.) appear largely resolved heading into the Sweet 16
Multiple expert bracket predictions (SBNation, Bleacher Report) favor UConn in this matchup, though Izzo's 4-3 historical record vs UConn gives MSU a credible path

result.text: 2026 March Madness Bracket Predictions – Nate Silver Subscribe Sign in Models & Forecasts 2026 March Madness Predictions Silver Bulletin odds for the men's NCAA tournament, powered by our new COOPER ratings. Nate Silver , Joseph George , and Eli McKown-Dawson Mar 18, 2026 ∙ Paid 108 31 14 Share See also: Women’s March Madness Predictions , Men’s COOPER ratings . 🏀 The latest March Madness projections Updated March 18, 2026 We’ve updated our projections with the results of tonight’s First Four games in Dayton. But almost every office pool starts on Thursday, once the field is reduced to 64 teams, so you still have plenty of time to fill out your picks. We also added new injury data earlier tonight. There were no major changes, but we now have a more pessimistic timeline for any return of Duke’s Caleb Foster. We also toned down the effect of injuries in an update last night. 1 We’ve set up the spreadsheet to include an archive of all previous predictions so you can see the effect of these changes. We’ll continue to update the projections as games are completed . -NS , 3/18/26 Sure, it might seem like the world’s gone a little crazy. But at least we’ll be getting a dose of March Madness. The NCAA tournament is perhaps our very favorite event here at Silver Bulletin. 2 In fact, the tournament really needs no introduction. I’ve been running versions of these projections since 2011, almost ten years before I’d even heard of Substack. But pretty much every part of the model has changed at some point . Instead of compiling other people’s projections, we now mostly rely on our own in the form of our new COOPER ratings — although we blend them with Ken Pomeroy’s ratings (because we think Pomeroy is pretty much the best, present company excluded.) We’ve also upgraded our technology stack. Believe it or not, until last year, part of the work for our NCAA model was still being done in Microsoft Excel. 3 Now we’ve built the code to run proper simulations — 100,000 at a time — which enables more sophisticated handling of injuries (and there are a lot of them this year), among other things. This isn’t an “AI-driven” model quite yet, though Claude and other AI tools are increasingly helping us smooth out some of the code's rough patches and turn these numbers around more quickly. Still, March Madness is eternal. If nothing else, there will be upsets: we’ve calculated that the odds of having a perfect bracket are on the order of 1 in 10 quintillion. I guess I’ll just have to concede that we’re living in a simulation if it happens. How our NCAA forecasts work This is what we call a landing page, meaning that both the numbers and the text will change. We strongly recommend checking out the web version with this link , which provides for better rendering of charts than the Substack app or email. We’ll update these numbers once per day after tournament games are played. In the interest of underpromising and hopefully overdelivering, sometimes the update will need to

result.text: 2026 March Madness Bracket Predictions – Nate Silver Subscribe Sign in Models & Forecasts 2026 March Madness Predictions Silver Bulletin odds for the men's NCAA tournament, powered by our new COOPER ratings. Nate Silver , Joseph George , and Eli McKown-Dawson Mar 18, 2026 ∙ Paid 108 31 14 Share See also: Women’s March Madness Predictions , Men’s COOPER ratings . 🏀 The latest March Madness projections Updated March 18, 2026 We’ve updated our projections with the results of tonight’s First Four games in Dayton. But almost every office pool starts on Thursday, once the field is reduced to 64 teams, so you still have plenty of time to fill out your picks. We also added new injury data earlier tonight. There were no major changes, but we now have a more pessimistic timeline for any return of Duke’s Caleb Foster. We also toned down the effect of injuries in an update last night. 1 We’ve set up the spreadsheet to include an archive of all previous predictions so you can see the effect of these changes. We’ll continue to update the projections as games are completed . -NS , 3/18/26 Sure, it might seem like the world’s gone a little crazy. But at least we’ll be getting a dose of March Madness. The NCAA tournament is perhaps our very favorite event here at Silver Bulletin. 2 In fact, the tournament really needs no introduction. I’ve been running versions of these projections since 2011, almost ten years before I’d even heard of Substack. But pretty much every part of the model has changed at some point . Instead of compiling other people’s projections, we now mostly rely on our own in the form of our new COOPER ratings — although we blend them with Ken Pomeroy’s ratings (because we think Pomeroy is pretty much the best, present company excluded.) We’ve also upgraded our technology stack. Believe it or not, until last year, part of the work for our NCAA model was still being done in Microsoft Excel. 3 Now we’ve built the code to run proper simulations — 100,000 at a time — which enables more sophisticated handling of injuries (and there are a lot of them this year), among other things. This isn’t an “AI-driven” model quite yet, though Claude and other AI tools are increasingly helping us smooth out some of the code's rough patches and turn these numbers around more quickly. Still, March Madness is eternal. If nothing else, there will be upsets: we’ve calculated that the odds of having a perfect bracket are on the order of 1 in 10 quintillion. I guess I’ll just have to concede that we’re living in a simulation if it happens. How our NCAA forecasts work This is what we call a landing page, meaning that both the numbers and the text will change. We strongly recommend checking out the web version with this link , which provides for better rendering of charts than the Substack app or email. We’ll update these numbers once per day after tournament games are played. In the interest of underpromising and hopefully overdelivering, sometimes the update will need to

  "result": [

      "id": "duke",

      "shortName": "DUKE",

      "shortName": "MSU",

      "region": "East",
      "conference": "Big Ten"
    },
    {
      "id": "kansas",
      "name": "Kansas",
      "shortName": "KU",
      "seed": 4,
      "region": "East",
      "conference": "Big 12"
    }
  ]
}

Now let me gather the key data points I already have from prior picks and research:

**Michigan State (3-seed)**:
- Record: 25-7
- SRS: 23.44 (10th nationally), SOS: 8th
- From prior picks: ORtg 117.3, DRtg 101.7
- Big Ten conference, Tom Izzo coaching
- Beat NDSU (R64) and Louisville (R32)

**UConn (2-seed)**:
- Record: 29-5
- SRS: 22.92 (12th nationally)
- KenPom #9, Torvik #9
- AdjEM: +29.0
- AdjO: ~123, AdjD: 94.0 (11th nationally)
- Dan Hurley coaching, chasing 3rd title in 4 years
- Jaylin Stewart injury concern (but expected back)
- Silas Demary Jr. "in pretty good shape"
- Beat Furman (R64) and UCLA (R32)
- Lost Big East Tournament championship to St. John's 72-52

Key matchup dynamics:
- UConn has a significantly higher adjusted efficiency margin (+29.0 vs roughly +15-16 for MSU based on ORtg/DRtg gap)
- UConn's elite defense (AdjD 94.0) vs MSU's average offense (ORtg 117.3)
- UConn's strong offense (~123 AdjO) vs MSU's below-average defense (DRtg 101.7)
- Multiple expert predictions favor UConn (SBNation, Bleacher Report), though one (Sporting News) gives slight edge to MSU based on Izzo's 4-3 record vs UConn

The efficiency gap is substantial - roughly 13 points per 100 possessions favoring UConn. UConn is elite on both ends while Michigan State's metrics are more modest for a 3-seed. The Big East Tournament loss to St. John's is a concern but was likely an outlier. UConn's health situation appears manageable with Demary healthy and Stewart potentially returning.

The Huskies' balanced attack on both ends of the floor—with a top-15 offense and top-11 defense—creates a significant two-way mismatch against a Michigan State team whose defensive rating of 101.7 will struggle to contain UConn's offensive firepower led by Tarris Reed Jr. and Alex Karaban. While Tom Izzo's legendary March pedigree and his 4-3 historical record against UConn give Michigan State a credible upset path, the quality gap is too wide for the Spartans to overcome, and UConn's health situation appears manageable with Silas Demary Jr. healthy and Jaylin Stewart expected to return."
